failure-modes/C57BL-6J-vs-N-substrain-co.title: C57BL/6J vs C57BL/6N substrain confounder

frontmatter.frequency_estimate: silent in any study that does not specify substrain (most common species-level "C57BL/6" claims)

failure-modes/C57BL-6J-vs-N-substrain-co.body_md:
# C57BL/6J vs C57BL/6N substrain confounder

## What it is

"C57BL/6" is not a single strain. The two dominant commercial substrains diverged in the 1950s:

- **C57BL/6J** (Jackson Laboratory, JAX 000664) — carries a 5-exon **Nnt** deletion that abolishes nicotinamide nucleotide transhydrogenase activity; documented effects on mitochondrial NADP(H) cycling, glucose homeostasis, oxidative-stress handling.
- **C57BL/6N** (Charles River / Taconic / others) — full-length Nnt; intact transhydrogenase activity.

In addition to *Nnt*, several other drift loci differ between substrains. Documented downstream phenotypic differences include glucose tolerance, behavioural responses, susceptibility to dietary stressors, and **baseline microbiome composition / barrier function**.

## How it manifests in barrier studies

- Two cohorts of "C57BL/6 mice" from different vendors (one JAX, one Charles River) are not equivalent. Baseline FD-4 plasma may differ measurably.
- Effect sizes of LGG (or any intervention) on barrier function may not transfer across substrains.
- Pooling literature across substrains in a meta-analysis is mixing two genotypes.

## How to detect it

- Read the methods carefully — papers that say "C57BL/6 mice (Charles River)" or "C57BL/6 mice (Jackson)" are usable; papers that just say "C57BL/6" are ambiguous.
- For prospective study design: pre-register **C57BL/6J** specifically with a single vendor.

## Mitigation

1. **Pre-register C57BL/6J from JAX 000664** (or 6N from Charles River) — stick to one substrain.
2. Single shipment, single age cohort.
3. When comparing your results to the literature, only weight studies that match your substrain.

failure-modes/FITC-dextran-dose-timing-h.title: FD-4 dose & blood-draw timing not standardised

frontmatter.frequency_estimate: visible across our entire FITC-dextran-flagged corpus

failure-modes/FITC-dextran-dose-timing-h.body_md:
# FD-4 dose & blood-draw timing not standardised

## What it is

Across the FITC-dextran-flagged papers in this wiki, dose ranges across **60–750 mg/kg** and blood-draw times across **1–4 h** post-gavage. The papers use plasma fluorescence as a continuous outcome but do not normalise for dose or timing — making direct effect-size comparisons across studies unreliable.

A 30 % reduction at 4 h post a 600 mg/kg gavage is not the same biological measurement as a 30 % reduction at 1 h post a 60 mg/kg gavage. Plasma FD-4 trajectory is a function of (absorption × distribution × clearance) and the integral changes shape with both dose and time-point.

## Why it matters for this hypothesis

The **30 % reduction threshold** in our hypothesis was specified without anchoring it to a specific protocol parameter. Any meta-analysis that pools "30 % reduction in plasma FD-4" across studies with different doses and times is mixing apples with pears. Pass 3 must:

1. **Pre-register a single dose / time-point** for the primary endpoint.
2. **Acknowledge in literature QC** that the published "LGG reduces gut permeability" effect-size literature is heterogeneous and any cross-study magnitude estimate is uncertain.

## Mitigation

1. Pick one dose × time pair and pre-register it. Recommended: **600 mg/kg, plasma at 4 h**.
2. Optionally collect two time-points (e.g. 1 h and 4 h) for AUC if budget allows — converts a snapshot to a kinetic measurement.
3. When citing prior literature in Pass 3, note dose and time when reporting effect sizes; do not pool across protocols.

failure-modes/FITC-dextran-photobleachin.title: FITC-dextran photobleaching during prep / read

frontmatter.frequency_estimate: not directly quantified, but detectable in any open-bench protocol

failure-modes/FITC-dextran-photobleachin.body_md:
# FITC-dextran photobleaching during prep / read

## What it is

Free FITC and FITC conjugates are photolabile under standard fluorescent lab lighting. The fluorescein chromophore loses ~5–15 % of its emission per hour of bench-top exposure under typical lab conditions, and substantially faster under direct lamp / window light. Plate-reader scans add small additional bleaching (negligible per scan but cumulative over re-reads).

The error is **direction-systematic**: bleached probe fluoresces less, so the measured plasma FD-4 concentration is **under**estimated. If the LGG arm samples are processed second (later in the day, more bench-time) than the controls, this can either mask or counterfeit a treatment effect.

## How it manifests

- Within-day signal drift: plate replicates run later in the day are systematically lower.
- Lower-than-expected absolute concentrations vs published.
- Effect-size attenuation: real differences shrink toward null.

## How to detect it

- Run a **FITC-dextran stability control**: one fixed-concentration well left in the dark and one left under typical lighting for the duration of the assay. If they differ by > 5 % at read time, you are bleaching.
- Test re-reading the same plate after 30 min on the bench.

## Mitigation

1. Wrap dosing syringes, reconstituted FD-4 stock, and plasma sample tubes in **aluminium foil** during all bench-top steps.
2. Keep reconstituted FD-4 at 4 °C in an opaque container.
3. Read the plate immediately after sample loading; do not let it sit on the deck.
4. **Randomise sample loading order** across treatment groups so any time-drift averages out across arms rather than biasing one direction.

failure-modes/LGG-strain-drift-passage.title: LGG genotypic / phenotypic drift on lab passage

frontmatter.frequency_estimate: cumulative — risk grows with each lab passage

failure-modes/LGG-strain-drift-passage.body_md:
# LGG genotypic / phenotypic drift on lab passage

## What it is

LGG (ATCC 53103) carries auxiliary genetic elements — including the SpaCBA pilus locus (msa) responsible for mucus adhesion — that are not strictly required for in vitro growth on MRS. Long lab passage selects against any element that costs metabolic effort but does not benefit growth. Documented consequences:

- **SpaCBA pilus loss** → reduced adhesion to mucus / Caco-2 → reduced colonisation in vivo.
- **EPS production reduction** → reduced barrier-protective postbiotic component.
- **Plasmid loss** more generally.

A "lab passage drifted LGG" is genotypically still ATCC 53103 but functionally a weaker probiotic — and the in vivo effect collapses without the experimenter realising why.

## How it manifests

- Lower adhesion in Caco-2 attachment assays vs sealed-master-stock LGG.
- Lower in vivo colonisation (faecal CFU per dose) than published values.
- Smaller barrier-protective effect in DSS or HFD models than literature predicts.
- Effects gradually fading across a multi-cohort study as the working stock ages.

## How to detect it

- Compare adhesion (Caco-2) of working stock vs ATCC sealed master vial.
- PCR-genotype for the SpaCBA locus on a sample of working-stock colonies before each major cohort.
- Run a "drift control": once a year, thaw a master-stock vial in parallel with current working stock and compare in vivo CFU per dose.

## Mitigation

1. Order a **fresh sealed ATCC 53103 vial** at study start.
2. Single expansion → single working-stock cryobank in 100+ aliquots; **never propagate beyond 5 passages** between cryostock and dosing.
3. Document passage number on every gavage day.
4. Discard working stock if drift is detected.

failure-modes/LGG-viability-loss-during-.title: LGG viability loss between thaw and gavage

frontmatter.frequency_estimate: a study that doesn't plate dose-day CFU is, by default, exposed to this failure mode

failure-modes/LGG-viability-loss-during-.body_md:
# LGG viability loss between thaw and gavage

## What it is

LGG viability decays measurably under typical bench-top conditions:

- ~0.5–1 log of CFU lost per hour at room temperature in PBS.
- Faster decay if resuspended in the wrong buffer (e.g. saline without buffer / nutrients).
- Substantial loss across a single freeze-thaw cycle if the cryostock was not glycerol-protected.

A study that thaws a glycerol stock at 8 a.m. and gavages the last cage at 2 p.m. has dosed a 3-log range of CFU across the cohort — without realising it.

## How it manifests

- Day-to-day or cage-to-cage variance in colonisation (faecal LGG CFU) that does not track with gavage volume.
- "Late" cages have lower colonisation than "early" cages.
- The intervention arm shows attenuated effect because the actual delivered dose is below design.

## How to detect it

- **Plate the gavage suspension at the moment of dosing** for every cage. Plot CFU vs cage order.
- If CFU declines monotonically with cage order, you have a viability problem — independent of how the gavage looks visually.

## Mitigation

1. **Prepare LGG fresh on the day of dosing**, not the night before.
2. Keep the gavage suspension on ice until the moment of dosing.
3. Plate **each cage's** gavage aliquot for CFU; reject any below 80 % of design dose.
4. Limit each prep to ≤ 90 min of bench time; if the cohort is larger, prep two batches.
5. Use 10 % skim-milk PBS (or trehalose-based protectant) as the suspension medium if very long bench time is unavoidable.

failure-modes/LPS-contamination-bacteria.title: Endotoxin / LPS contamination of LGG prep

frontmatter.frequency_estimate: persistent low-level confounder in any in-house bacterial / recombinant-protein prep

failure-modes/LPS-contamination-bacteria.body_md:
# Endotoxin / LPS contamination of LGG prep

## What it is

LGG itself is gram-positive and does not produce LPS. But MRS broth ingredients (peptones, yeast extract) can carry trace endotoxin, and lab water / glassware can introduce more. A typical lab-grown LGG prep can carry 10–1000 EU/mL of LPS unless explicitly endotoxin-managed.

For **recombinant p40 / p75 expressed in *E. coli*** the contamination problem is much worse — the protein is co-purified with abundant *E. coli* LPS unless polymyxin B columns or ToxinEraser-style cleanup is used.

## Why it matters

- Plasma LPS / LBP readings post-LGG dosing may reflect the **carrier**, not the **intervention** — confounding the very translocation marker we use.
- Cytokine responses (TNF-α, IL-6) attributed to LGG / p40 may be LPS-driven via TLR4.
- Effect sizes from in-house p40 preps may not replicate with truly endotoxin-free recombinant p40.

## How to detect it

- LAL-quantify endotoxin in every batch of LGG suspension and recombinant protein. Reject if > 1 EU/mL for in vivo use.
- Run a polymyxin-B-treated control (binds and neutralises LPS): if the polymyxin-treated arm loses the cytokine response, you were measuring LPS.

## Mitigation

1. **Endotoxin-grade water and consumables** for LGG suspension prep.
2. Wash LGG cell pellet ≥ 3× in endotoxin-low PBS before resuspension.
3. For recombinant p40: polymyxin B affinity column + LAL re-test before use.
4. Quantify endotoxin per batch and report it.

failure-modes/ZO-1-occludin-detection-ar.title: Tight-junction protein detection artefacts

failure-modes/ZO-1-occludin-detection-ar.body_md:
# Tight-junction protein detection artefacts

## What it is

Antibody-based quantification of ZO-1, occludin, and the claudin family is harder than the methods sections suggest. Pervasive issues:

- **Antibody clone variability**: different commercial clones for the "same" protein give different bands at different molecular weights, with different specificity. Claudin antibodies are notorious.
- **Total vs membrane pool**: WB on whole lysate measures total protein; what matters for barrier function is **membrane-localised** protein at the tight junction. Total ZO-1 can be unchanged while membrane ZO-1 drops by half — a real barrier change WB will miss.
- **Loading-control drift in colitis**: GAPDH and β-actin can themselves shift with inflammation. Total-protein normalisation (Ponceau, stain-free) is more robust.
- **IF quantification subjectivity**: line-scan-based "intensity at the apical junction" is operator-dependent without blinded analysis or automated pipelines.

## How it manifests

- WB shows no change but IF shows redistribution → real barrier change misclassified as "no effect."
- Different antibody lot mid-study creates an apparent treatment effect that's actually a reagent change.
- LGG arm shows "increased ZO-1" because the LGG-arm gels were loaded slightly differently.

## Mitigation

1. **Blind the analyst** to treatment when scoring IF.
2. Use **automated quantification pipelines** (e.g. CellProfiler ZO-1 line scans) over manual scoring.
3. **Pair WB with IF on adjacent sections** — agreement increases confidence; disagreement is informative.
4. Use a single antibody lot for the whole study; document catalog + lot number.
5. Use **total-protein normalisation** instead of GAPDH / β-actin in DSS / inflammation contexts.
6. Include positive-control tissue (treated with a known barrier disruptor) on every blot.

failure-modes/baseline-permeability-too-.title: Baseline permeability too low to detect a 30 % drop

frontmatter.frequency_estimate: the headline risk for this specific hypothesis

failure-modes/baseline-permeability-too-.body_md:
# Baseline permeability too low to detect a 30 % drop

## What it is

Healthy adult C57BL/6J mice have **low and tightly distributed plasma FD-4** at baseline. Published values cluster around 0.5–2 µg/mL at 4 h post a 600 mg/kg gavage. The **assay floor** (sample autofluorescence, plate-reader noise, residual matrix effects) is typically 0.1–0.3 µg/mL.

A 30 % reduction from a baseline of 1 µg/mL is 0.3 µg/mL — **the same magnitude as the assay floor**. It is mathematically possible to detect with very large n and tight technique, but in practice most published unstressed-baseline studies fail to find anything.

This is the **headline risk** for the hypothesis as written. It is why the corpus is dominated by challenge models (DSS, alcohol, HFD, NEC) — those models elevate baseline FD-4 by 5–20×, making a 30 % LGG effect easy to detect.

## How it manifests

- Power calculation shows n > 30 / arm needed even at modest within-group SD.
- Pilot data shows mean LGG plasma FD-4 lower than mean PBS — but not statistically significantly so.
- Effect-size estimate is dominated by 1–2 outlier mice.

## How to detect it

- Pre-study power calculation using:
  - SD from a published unstressed C57BL/6 cohort
  - The 30 % effect size
  - α = 0.05, power = 0.80
  - If n > 15 / arm, the design is at-risk.
- Pilot study (n = 4 / arm) to estimate within-group SD before committing to the main cohort.

## Mitigation (Pass 3 will choose between)

1. **Add a barrier-challenge arm** (mild DSS, 1 % × 5 d, or HFD × 8 wk) — primary endpoint becomes "% reduction in challenge-induced FD-4 elevation." Easier to power, more biologically informative.
2. **Use plasma LBP / LPS as primary endpoint** ([[../methods/LPS-endotoxin-quantification]]) — has lower baseline noise relative to dynamic range in unstressed animals.
3. **Increase n per arm** to 20–30 — costly but defensible.
4. **Re-frame hypothesis** to "≥ 30 % reduction in challenge-induced FD-4" — more specific, more powered, more publishable.

failure-modes/challenge-model-vs-baselin.title: Published LGG-barrier evidence comes from challenge models, not baseline mice

frontmatter.frequency_estimate: applies to literature-comparison for this hypothesis specifically

failure-modes/challenge-model-vs-baselin.body_md:
# Published LGG-barrier evidence comes from challenge models, not baseline mice

## What it is

The hypothesis pre-registers the LGG-permeability test in **unchallenged adult C57BL/6 mice**. Reviewing our 72-source corpus, ~85 % of the LGG-permeability evidence is from **challenge models** — DSS colitis, alcohol-induced leaky gut, HFD-induced metabolic endotoxaemia, NEC, post-weaning stress, ageing, irradiation, chemotherapy.

The published "LGG protects intestinal barrier" effect is robust **in challenged mice**. Generalising it to unchallenged C57BL/6 — where the barrier is already intact — is an unsupported extrapolation.

This pairs with [[baseline-permeability-too-low-detect-30pct]]: not only is the baseline near the assay floor, but the literature you would cite to predict an effect doesn't actually study your animals.

## How it manifests in literature QC

- Pass 3's literature-QC step would say "similar-work-exists" for LGG + barrier studies, but in fact every cited paper uses a different baseline state. The "exact match" is somewhere between "not-found" and "similar-work-exists with major caveats."
- Effect-size predictions from the literature are inflated by the challenge state of those studies.

## How to detect it

- Tabulate every paper you cite for LGG → permeability with a column "baseline state" — DSS / alcohol / HFD / NEC / unchallenged.
- Count the unchallenged-baseline rows. If < 5, the literature does not support the hypothesis as stated.

## Mitigation

1. Re-frame hypothesis to include a challenge (Pass 3 decision).
2. Or pre-register that this is **a deliberately under-explored question** and that null result is informative.
3. Or use a more sensitive primary endpoint than FD-4 (LBP, multi-probe).
4. Make the framing transparent in the literature-QC verdict — the brief asks for honest novelty assessment.

failure-modes/coprophagy-microbiome-equi.title: Coprophagy equilibrates microbiomes across co-housed cages

frontmatter.frequency_estimate: cohort-level — silently destroys the LGG-vs-control contrast in any co-housed design

failure-modes/coprophagy-microbiome-equi.body_md:
# Coprophagy equilibrates microbiomes across co-housed cages

## What it is

Mice eat each other's faeces (coprophagy) and groom each other extensively. In a shared cage, this means the microbiome of all cage-mates **converges within days**. Even on a shared rack with shared ventilation, faecal aerosol can transfer Lactobacilli between cages.

For an LGG-vs-PBS comparison, this is the difference between:
- **Real test**: LGG cage colonises with LGG; PBS cage stays at baseline → real contrast.
- **Coprophagy collapse**: LGG cage colonises with LGG; PBS cage acquires LGG via shared rack air or attendant cross-contamination → no contrast → null result, no matter how good the intervention.

The classic Th17-Treg studies that established the JAX-vs-Taconic SFB story had to use **separate isolators** to maintain the difference, because co-housed mice equilibrate within ~3 weeks.

## How it manifests

- Faecal LGG CFU detected in the supposed control arm.
- Within-arm variance smaller than between-arm — opposite of expectation.
- Real intervention effect appears null.

## How to detect it

- **Faecal CFU on MRS-anaerobic plates** weekly from a sentinel mouse in each control cage. If LGG appears, contamination has happened.
- Strain-specific qPCR (LGG *welE* primers) on a faecal aliquot from each cage at end of study.

## Mitigation

1. **House LGG and control arms in separate cages** — never co-house treatment with control.
2. Use **separate ventilated racks** if possible; minimum, alternate cages on the rack with an empty cage between treatment and control.
3. Dedicated technician PPE / gloves changed between LGG and control cage handling.
4. Bottom-up changing order: control cages first, then LGG cages, in cage-change cycles.
5. Pre-register the housing design and report it in methods.

failure-modes/incomplete-fasting-before-.title: Incomplete fasting before FITC-dextran gavage

frontmatter.frequency_estimate: top documented source of inter-laboratory variance in FITC-dextran intestinal permeability assays

failure-modes/incomplete-fasting-before-.body_md:
# Incomplete fasting before FITC-dextran gavage

## What it is

Food in the stomach at the time of FD-4 gavage **dilutes** the dose, **delays** gastric emptying, and **adsorbs** a fraction of the probe onto food matrix. The same nominal dose (mg/kg) reaches the small-intestinal lumen later and at lower effective concentration. Plasma kinetics shift right; a single late time-point is the most affected.

In the corpus, fasting protocols range across:
- **No fast** (a few studies) — uninterpretable in our framework
- **2-h fast** — partial; mice are largely nocturnal feeders, so daytime 2 h is mostly a no-op
- **4-h fast** — the most common
- **Overnight fast (12–14 h)** — over-stresses the mouse, alters the microbiome, and is ethically borderline; not recommended for routine permeability work

A 6-h daytime fast is the practical sweet spot.

## How it manifests

- Within-cohort plasma FD-4 variance balloons (mice that found a food crumb in their cage have a different absorption profile).
- Cross-study comparisons with mismatched fasting protocols are not meaningful.
- LGG-vs-control differences can be swamped by within-arm fasting noise.

## How to detect it

- Visual inspection: fasted mouse cages should have empty hoppers and clean bedding (no scattered food). Confirm at gavage time.
- A **postprandial control arm** (n = 3 fed mice gavaged FD-4) read alongside the main cohort: if the fed-arm plasma FD-4 differs > 50 % from the fasted controls, fasting compliance was the issue.

## Mitigation

1. **Pre-register a 6-h daytime fast**, water ad lib, identical for all arms.
2. **Remove food + visible crumbs** from cages 6 h ± 15 min before gavage.
3. Confirm cage cleanliness at gavage time.
4. Document any deviations per cage; exclude any obviously-fed mouse from the assay (with reason logged).

failure-modes/positive-publication-bias-.title: Strong positive-publication bias in LGG-barrier literature

frontmatter.frequency_estimate: pervasive in the probiotic-barrier literature; multiple confirmed retractions of LGG papers

failure-modes/positive-publication-bias-.body_md:
# Strong positive-publication bias in LGG-barrier literature

## What it is

The published LGG-barrier literature is heavily skewed positive. Multiple converging signals:

1. **Direct retractions of LGG-mouse papers in our corpus** ([[../sources/2014-singh-lgg-fermented-milk-aging-mice-RETRACTED]], [[../sources/2024-retraction-notice-singh-lgg-aging-mice]], [[../sources/2021-lgg-conditioned-medium-spinal-cord-injury-RETRACTED]], [[../sources/2020-masia-lgg-fermented-plant-RETRACTED]], [[../sources/2013-petrillo-lgasseri-sf1183-epithelial-RETRACTED]]). The 2014 Singh aging-mouse retraction is *especially* relevant — its claims overlap our intervention-outcome combination almost exactly.
2. **OpenAlex snapshot** ([[../sources/openalex-lactobacillus-retractions-snapshot]]): 36 retracted Lactobacillus papers across the broader literature, a non-trivial fraction of which have probiotic-barrier claims.
3. **Underpowered designs** are the norm — many papers in the corpus have n = 4–8 / arm.
4. **Unstressed-baseline arms with null results are largely absent** from the published record. The "challenge-model" preference (see [[challenge-model-vs-baseline-mismatch]]) is partly biological and partly publication bias — nulls in unstressed mice don't get written up.

## Why it matters for this hypothesis

- The "30 % reduction" effect-size threshold is implicitly anchored to published LGG-barrier effect sizes. Those sizes are inflated by publication bias.
- A real-world LGG-barrier effect in unstressed mice may be much smaller than the meta-mean of published challenge-model studies.
- Pass 3's literature-QC verdict needs to weight the evidence accordingly.

## How to detect it

- Funnel plot of effect size vs sample size across studies that report both → asymmetry indicates bias.
- Compare Cochrane / systematic-review effect estimates (which down-weight small studies) to the headline mean — large gap = bias.
- Search for and cite explicit null-result papers; if you can't find any, that itself is the signal.

## Mitigation

1. **Cite the retractions** explicitly in Pass 3's literature-QC verdict.
2. Discount published effect sizes by ~30–50 % when forecasting your own expected effect.
3. **Pre-register the design and analysis** of this study in advance (OSF or similar) — every line you commit to publicly is one less degree of freedom for post-hoc cherry-picking.
4. **Commit to publishing the result** regardless of direction, including null. Make this explicit in the IACUC protocol and any preregistration.
5. In the lab brief, flag the Singh 2014 retraction as a direct precedent that did *not* hold up.

## Sources mentioning this

- [[../sources/openalex-lactobacillus-retractions-snapshot|2026 · OpenAlex — snapshot of all retracted Lactobacillus papers (n = 36)]]
- [[../sources/2024-retraction-notice-singh-lgg-aging-mice|2024 · Singer Springer — Retraction Note: Singh et al. LGG fermented milk in aging mice]]
- [[../sources/2021-lgg-conditioned-medium-spinal-cord-injury-RETRACTED|2021 · Lin — [Retracted] LGG conditioned medium / spinal cord injury / microglia polarisation]]
- [[../sources/2020-masia-lgg-fermented-plant-RETRACTED|2020 · Masiá — RETRACTED: L. rhamnosus on physicochemical properties of fermented plant-based materials]]
- [[../sources/2020-retraction-notice-masia-lgg-plant|2020 · Editor — Retraction notice for Masiá et al. 2020]]
- [[../sources/2014-singh-lgg-fermented-milk-aging-mice-RETRACTED|2014 · Singh — RETRACTED ARTICLE: LGG fermented milk improves Th1/Th2 / antioxidative status in aging mice]]
- [[../sources/2013-petrillo-lgasseri-sf1183-epithelial-RETRACTED|2013 · Petrillo — RETRACTED: L. gasseri SF1183 affects intestinal epithelial cell survival and growth]]

failure-modes/probiotic-bacteremia-sepsi.title: Probiotic bacteraemia / sepsis (rare but serious)

frontmatter.frequency_estimate: rare in healthy adult mice; documented in immunocompromised, preterm, and barrier-injured hosts

failure-modes/probiotic-bacteremia-sepsi.body_md:
# Probiotic bacteraemia / sepsis (rare but serious)

## What it is

Live LGG can translocate from the gut lumen to the bloodstream in hosts whose barrier is severely compromised, whose immune defence is reduced, or both. This is a documented clinical adverse event in:

- Preterm neonates (Farella et al. 2025, [[../sources/2025-farella-lactobacillus-rhamnosus-sepsis-preterm-infant]] — sepsis case in a preterm infant after probiotic administration)
- Immunocompromised oncology patients
- Patients with central venous catheters
- Patients with major gut barrier breach (severe IBD, post-surgical, mucositis)

## Relevance to this hypothesis

- The intervention is in **healthy adult immunocompetent C57BL/6**. Sepsis risk is very low in this population.
- However: any animal that develops unexpected illness (lethargy, weight loss > 20 %, ruffled coat) **must** be excluded and considered for blood culture.
- IACUC review should explicitly note the LGG sepsis literature and require euthanasia criteria.

## Detection

- **Daily welfare scoring** (body weight, posture, coat, activity) for the full 28 d.
- Blood culture on any animal meeting humane-endpoint criteria, before euthanasia.
- Post-mortem: if any animal dies unexpectedly, send blood + spleen + liver for culture before discarding.

## Mitigation

1. Use immunocompetent adult animals only — exclude immunocompromised lines from this protocol.
2. No intervention in animals with concurrent injury / surgery.
3. Pre-defined humane endpoint criteria in IACUC protocol.
4. Adverse-event reporting plan.

## Sources mentioning this

failure-modes/serum-autofluorescence-FIT.title: Serum autofluorescence inflates FITC-dextran readout

frontmatter.frequency_estimate: ~30 % of in vivo FITC-dextran studies that report effect sizes

failure-modes/serum-autofluorescence-FIT.body_md:
# Serum autofluorescence inflates FITC-dextran readout

## What it is

Mouse plasma is not optically clean. Free haemoglobin (haemolysis from blood draw), bilirubin (jaundice from challenge models), lipaemia (postprandial samples), and endogenous flavoproteins all fluoresce or absorb in the FITC band (485 nm ex / 520 nm em). The blank plasma signal is non-trivial — typically 5–15 % of a positive FD-4 reading.

If the standard curve is built in **buffer** (PBS, water) rather than **pooled naïve plasma**, the unknowns are read against a baseline that excludes this matrix fluorescence. Net result: **systematic overestimation of plasma FD-4** in all samples, with the absolute error proportional to plasma autofluorescence.

## How it manifests

- Treatment effects vanish when controls are also high (LGG and PBS arms both have inflated readings against the wrong blank).
- Inter-cohort variance balloons — different blood-draw quality across days produces different baseline noise.
- A 30 % real reduction in paracellular FD-4 can be invisible if the blank floor is high.

## How to detect it

- Pre-calibrate: compare standard curves built in PBS vs in pooled naïve plasma. If the slopes differ by > 20 %, you are matrix-sensitive.
- Run a **plasma-only blank well** every plate: if it reads above ~5 % of your highest standard, autofluorescence is non-negligible.

## Mitigation

1. **Always** build the FD-4 standard curve in **pooled plasma from naïve, age-matched mice**, not in buffer.
2. Read on a black 96-well plate (white plates over-reflect).
3. Avoid haemolysed samples — score visually; reject any visibly pink plasma.
4. Collect samples consistently from the same vein at the same time-of-day.
5. Standardise fasting (a postprandial draw is lipaemic).

failure-modes/vendor-of-origin-microbiom.title: Vendor-of-origin microbiome shifts baseline barrier
failure-modes/vendor-of-origin-microbiom.body_md:
# Vendor-of-origin microbiome shifts baseline barrier

## What it is

Mice from different commercial vendors (Jackson, Charles River, Taconic, Envigo) arrive with measurably different commensal communities — even at identical genotype and age. The differences are driven by colony husbandry, dam-derived seeding, water and feed differences across breeding facilities. Documented impact on:

- DSS colitis susceptibility
- Th17 / Treg balance (the famous Taconic vs JAX SFB story)
- Baseline endotoxaemia
- Probiotic engraftment efficiency (LGG colonises differently against different background communities)

## Why it matters for this hypothesis

- An LGG niche may already be occupied (high baseline *Lactobacillus*) or empty (low baseline) depending on the source colony, changing how easily LGG engrafts.
- Baseline FD-4 plasma levels — your "control" — depend on the background microbiome. Two control groups from two vendors are not the same baseline.
- Animals shipped recently arrive in a transient state (stress + diet change + microbiome dysbiosis); see acclimation note below.

## Mitigation

1. **Single vendor, single shipment, single age cohort** for the entire study.
2. ≥ 14 d acclimation in the local vivarium before any intervention starts — enough for the microbiome to re-equilibrate.
3. Document the source colony in methods; compare your effect sizes only to literature using the same vendor when possible.
4. Optionally pre-screen baseline microbiome with a single faecal 16S sample from each cage before randomisation; balance LGG vs control arms by baseline community PCA.

## Sources mentioning this

# Wiki index — *Lactobacillus rhamnosus* GG and C57BL/6 intestinal permeability

Entry point for the wiki built by Pass 2 from `raw/`. Open the entire `groundwork/` directory as a single **Obsidian vault** to use the graph view.

## Hypothesis

- [[../hypothesis|hypothesis.md]] — root node: refined intervention / outcome / threshold / mechanism / control

## Methods (13)

- [[methods/16S-rRNA-microbiome-sequencing]] — Taxonomic profiling of the gut microbiota community via amplicon sequencing of variable regions of the bacterial 16S rRNA gene (typically V3
- [[methods/alcohol-induced-leaky-gut-model]] — Acute or chronic ethanol exposure used to model alcoholic liver disease and the gut hyperpermeability that precedes it. Two dominant variant
- [[methods/bacterial-CFU-enumeration]] — Viable-cell counting by serial dilution and plating on selective agar. The **dosing-QC backbone** of any probiotic study — without it, you d
- [[methods/DSS-colitis-model]] — Standard chemical model of acute / chronic colonic epithelial injury. DSS is added to the drinking water (typically **2–3 % w/v for 5–7 days
- [[methods/ELISA-cytokine-quantification]] — Plate-based immunoassay for plasma / mucosal cytokines and barrier-translocation markers. In our context, the relevant analytes are:
- [[methods/ex-vivo-intestinal-permeability-assay]] — Fresh mucosal explant assays in which a defined area of intestine is mounted between donor (luminal) and acceptor (serosal) chambers, FD-4 i
- [[methods/FITC-dextran-permeability-assay]] — The single-most-used in vivo readout of small-molecule paracellular gut barrier function. A defined dose of fluorescein-conjugated dextran (
- [[methods/high-fat-diet-model]] — Mice fed 45–60 % kcal-from-fat purified diets for 8–16 weeks develop diet-induced obesity, metabolic endotoxaemia, and a measurable, slow-on
- [[methods/LPS-endotoxin-quantification]] — Two complementary platforms:
- [[methods/oral-gavage-mouse]] — Direct intragastric delivery of a defined volume / dose using a curved, ball-tipped feeding needle. The standard route for both the **probio
- [[methods/SCFA-quantification]] — GC-FID or HPLC measurement of acetate, propionate, butyrate (the canonical trio) and the C5 / C6 branched-chain SCFAs in caecal content, fae
- [[methods/Ussing-chamber-TEER]] — Ex vivo electrophysiological measurement of mucosal barrier integrity. Freshly excised intestinal tissue (or a polarised cell-monolayer) is 
- [[methods/Western-blot-tight-junction]] — Protein-level quantification of the canonical tight-junction (TJ) cassette: **ZO-1** (zonula occludens-1, scaffolding), **occludin**, **clau

## Reagents (8)

- [[reagents/DSS]] — Sulfated polysaccharide (sulfate density ~17 %, MW typically 36–50 kDa for mouse colitis work). Dosed in drinking water at 2–3 % w/v for 5–7
- [[reagents/FITC-dextran-4kDa]] — The canonical small-molecule paracellular tracer for in vivo intestinal permeability. A 4 kDa dextran polymer randomly substituted with fluo
- [[reagents/LGG-exopolysaccharide-EPS]] — The polysaccharide capsule layer LGG secretes. Two operationally distinct fractions:
- [[reagents/LGG-extracellular-vesicles]] — Membrane-bounded particles (~50–200 nm) shed by LGG into the surrounding medium. Wang et al. 2025 (PMC12360853) characterise LGG-EVs contain
- [[reagents/LPS]] — Two distinct uses in this corpus:
- [[reagents/MRS-broth]] — The standard liquid growth medium for *Lactobacillus*. Selective via low pH (~6.5 reducing during growth), ammonium / Tween 80 / acetate buf
- [[reagents/p40-LGG-derived-protein]] — A ~40 kDa soluble protein secreted by *L. rhamnosus* GG. The most thoroughly mechanistically dissected LGG-derived molecule in our corpus. D
- [[reagents/p75-Msp2-LGG-derived]] — A second LGG-secreted soluble protein (~75 kDa) with epithelial cytoprotective activity, characterised alongside p40 by the Yan/Polk lab. Ac

## Organisms (5)

- [[organisms/C57BL-6]] — The hypothesis pre-registers **C57BL/6J** specifically (JAX 000664) — not the generic "C57BL/6" — for two reasons:
- [[organisms/Caco-2]] — Caco-2 monolayers on Transwell inserts are the in vitro workhorse for tight-junction biology — TEER, FD-4 flux, claudin localisation under c
- [[organisms/HeLa]] — Mentioned in tangential contexts in this corpus (e.g. Domínguez-Díaz 2023 uses HaCaT keratinocytes — not HeLa — to show recombinant p40 indu
- [[organisms/lactobacillus-rhamnosus-GG]] — The most thoroughly studied probiotic *Lactobacillus*, isolated by Sherwood Gorbach and Barry Goldin in 1985 from a healthy adult human stoo
- [[organisms/lactobacillus-rhamnosus-other-strains]] — Other *L. rhamnosus* strains appear scattered through our corpus in two contexts:

methods/16S-rRNA-microbiome-sequencing.title: 16S rRNA gene sequencing & qPCR
methods/16S-rRNA-microbiome-sequencing.body_md:
# 16S rRNA gene sequencing & qPCR

Taxonomic profiling of the gut microbiota community via amplicon sequencing of variable regions of the bacterial 16S rRNA gene (typically V3–V4 on Illumina MiSeq). For LGG specifically, **strain-level resolution is not achievable from short-read 16S** — quantification of LGG itself requires LGG-specific qPCR primers (e.g. targeting the *welE* gene) or shotgun metagenomics.

## Why we need it for this hypothesis

- Confirm the LGG arm actually shows elevated *Lactobacillus* relative abundance vs control (engraftment check).
- Confirm controls are NOT cross-contaminated (rules out the [[../failure-modes/coprophagy-microbiome-equilibration]] failure mode).
- Detect any global community shift that might confound the LGG-permeability attribution.

## Canonical parameters

- **DNA extraction**: bead-beating (Qiagen DNeasy PowerSoil) — the only robust extraction method for gram-positive Lactobacilli.
- **Region**: V3–V4 (~460 bp).
- **Sequencing depth**: ≥ 30 000 reads/sample.
- **LGG quantification**: orthogonal qPCR with LGG-specific primer set on a parallel aliquot.

## Sources mentioning this

- [[../sources/2026-zhu-integrative-mendelian-randomization-experimental-validation|2026 · Zhu — Integrative Mendelian randomization and experimental validation prioritize KLF4 in the gut]]
- [[../sources/2026-yu-long-noncoding-rna-gas5-disrupts|2026 · Yu — Long noncoding RNA  GAS5  disrupts intestinal epithelial barrier function by increasing sm]]

methods/DSS-colitis-model.title: DSS-induced colitis (mouse)
methods/DSS-colitis-model.body_md:
# DSS-induced colitis (mouse)

Standard chemical model of acute / chronic colonic epithelial injury. DSS is added to the drinking water (typically **2–3 % w/v for 5–7 days**) and disrupts the colonic epithelium by direct cytotoxicity to colonocytes plus mucin damage. Recovery, weight loss, disease activity index, and barrier breakdown are the standard readouts.

## Relevance to this hypothesis

The hypothesis is about **baseline** intestinal permeability in unchallenged C57BL/6 mice — but **the bulk of the LGG-barrier evidence in our corpus comes from DSS models, not unchallenged mice**. Pass 3 must decide whether to:

1. Stay strictly with the unchallenged design (and accept the floor-effect risk — see [[../failure-modes/baseline-permeability-too-low-detect-30pct]]), or
2. Add a mild DSS arm (1 % × 5 d) as a positive control to confirm the FD-4 assay is sensitive in this animal cohort, or
3. Re-frame the hypothesis around a barrier-perturbed mouse and use DSS as the primary stressor.

The cross-source pattern is unambiguous: **published LGG → barrier effects in DSS mice are robust** (Si 2025, Chen 2025, Suntornsaratoon 2024, Liu 2025); **published LGG → barrier effects in unstressed adult mice are sparse and underpowered**.

## Canonical parameters

| Parameter | Typical |
|---|---|
| DSS molecular weight | 36–50 kDa (potency depends strongly on this; report lot) |
| Concentration | 2–3 % w/v in autoclaved drinking water |
| Duration | 5–7 d for acute; cyclic 3-cycle protocol for chronic |
| Endpoint | day 7–10: body weight, DAI, colon length, FD-4, histology |

## Common pitfalls

- **DSS lot variability**: different MW distributions and supplier lots give substantially different disease severity. Pre-test each lot.
- **Strain susceptibility**: C57BL/6J is more DSS-susceptible than BALB/c.
- **Microbiome dependence**: DSS severity drops in germ-free mice and varies with vendor microbiome. See [[../failure-modes/vendor-of-origin-microbiome-baseline]].

methods/ELISA-cytokine-quantification.title: ELISA cytokine and barrier-marker quantification
methods/ELISA-cytokine-quantification.body_md:
# ELISA cytokine and barrier-marker quantification

Plate-based immunoassay for plasma / mucosal cytokines and barrier-translocation markers. In our context, the relevant analytes are:

- **TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β** — pro-inflammatory; rise with barrier loss
- **IL-10** — anti-inflammatory; LGG mechanism (Si 2025: STING-dependent IL-10)
- **LBP (LPS-binding protein)** — systemic translocation surrogate; preferred over plasma LPS itself for chronic studies
- **Lipocalin-2 / faecal calprotectin** — luminal inflammation markers

## Recommended platform

For a small mouse study (n ≤ 16/arm), single-analyte ELISAs are fine. For mechanism dissection across many cytokines, a **multiplex Luminex or MSD panel** is more efficient and uses ~25 µL plasma/sample (relevant when mouse blood volumes are limiting).

methods/FITC-dextran-permeability-assay.title: FITC-dextran intestinal permeability assay
methods/FITC-dextran-permeability-assay.body_md:
# FITC-dextran intestinal permeability assay

The single-most-used in vivo readout of small-molecule paracellular gut barrier function. A defined dose of fluorescein-conjugated dextran (typically 4 kDa, "FD-4") is delivered by oral gavage to fasted mice; plasma is sampled and the fluorescence translated to dextran concentration against a same-day standard curve. The reading is the **primary endpoint** in our hypothesis.

## What it measures

- **Paracellular flux through the small-intestinal epithelium**, dominated by the leak/unrestricted pathway (which 4 kDa molecules can use when ZO-1/occludin/claudin barriers are compromised). FD-4 does **not** report on transcellular permeability or on large-molecule (FD-40, FD-70) translocation, both of which require companion probes if the mechanism question demands them.
- It is a **single-time-point integral** of luminal-to-blood passage between gavage and sample. Unlike Ussing-chamber TEER, it is not a real-time conductance measurement.

## Canonical protocol (consensus from sources)

| Step | Parameters seen in literature | Recommended (this hypothesis) |
|---|---|---|
| Fast | 4–6 h (most), water ad lib; some studies use no fast | **6 h fast**, water ad lib (standardise) |
| FD-4 dose | 60 mg / 100 g BW (Volynets-style) … 600 mg/kg … up to 750 mg/kg | **600 mg/kg** in sterile PBS |
| Vehicle | PBS, sterile water, saline | sterile PBS |
| Gavage volume | 100–200 µL via 22G stainless ball-tip needle | 200 µL |
| Plasma draw | retro-orbital, submandibular, or terminal cardiac | submandibular at 4 h post-gavage |
| Read | 485 nm ex / 520 nm em on a fluorescent plate reader | same; 96-well black plate |
| Quant | external FD-4 standard curve in **pooled naïve mouse plasma** (not buffer) — corrects for matrix autofluorescence | required |

## Common variants

- **Larger probes** (FD-40, FD-70): used when leak-path damage is suspected; not paracellular-specific.
- **Multi-probe (lactulose / mannitol / sucralose) with urine collection**: dispenses with terminal blood draw and resolves small- vs large-bowel permeability separately. Out of scope here.
- **Ex vivo X-IPA** (Aldars-García 2023): ex vivo correlate; useful for mechanism dissection on isolated mucosa.

## Things to watch for

- The standard curve **must** be made in the same biological matrix (plasma) and read on the same plate as samples — buffer-only standards systematically bias the reading. See [[../failure-modes/serum-autofluorescence-FITC-readout]].
- Sample handling **must** be in amber tubes / shielded from ambient light. See [[../failure-modes/FITC-dextran-photobleaching]].
- Fasting status is the single largest source of inter-study variance. See [[../failure-modes/incomplete-fasting-before-gavage]].
- The published dose / timing matrix is wide enough that effect-size meta-analysis across studies is unreliable. See [[../failure-modes/FITC-dextran-dose-timing-heterogeneity]].

## How it relates to alternative methods

- **TEER / Ussing chamber** ([[Ussing-chamber-TEER]]) is the orthogonal mucosal-conductance measurement. They usually agree on direction but not magnitude.
- **Plasma LBP / endotoxin** ([[LPS-endotoxin-quantification]]) is the systemic-translocation surrogate. It can detect translocation events FD-4 misses (large-molecule, late-phase).
- **Tight-junction protein quantification** ([[Western-blot-tight-junction]]) gives the molecular substrate for any FD-4 change.

methods/LPS-endotoxin-quantification.title: Plasma LPS / endotoxin quantification
methods/LPS-endotoxin-quantification.body_md:
# Plasma LPS / endotoxin quantification

Two complementary platforms:

- **LAL (Limulus amoebocyte lysate)** — direct LPS measurement; gold standard but exquisitely sensitive to environmental contamination (single fingerprint = false positive).
- **LBP ELISA** — plasma LPS-binding protein as an integrated proxy for translocation over hours-days. More robust to handling, less time-resolved.

## Why pair with FITC-dextran

FD-4 reports small-molecule paracellular flux at one timepoint. LBP reports systemic gram-negative-derived translocation integrated over recent days. If LGG suppresses FD-4 but LBP is unchanged, the barrier improvement is restricted to the small-molecule pathway. If both move together, the effect is broader.

methods/SCFA-quantification.title: Short-chain fatty acid (SCFA) quantification
methods/SCFA-quantification.body_md:
# Short-chain fatty acid (SCFA) quantification

GC-FID or HPLC measurement of acetate, propionate, butyrate (the canonical trio) and the C5 / C6 branched-chain SCFAs in caecal content, faeces, or portal-vein plasma.

## Why this is mechanistically important

Multiple sources link the LGG-barrier effect to SCFA production — directly (LGG produces lactate and acetate, cross-fed to butyrate-producers) or indirectly (LGG colonisation shifts community composition toward butyrogens). Butyrate is the preferred energy substrate of colonocytes and stabilises HIF-1α to maintain barrier function.

## Canonical parameters

- Sample: caecal content, ~50 mg, snap-frozen at sacrifice, processed within 30 d.
- Internal standard: 2-ethylbutyric acid.
- Column: ZB-FFAP or similar polar capillary GC.
- Reporting: µmol/g caecal content (preferred over % composition).

methods/Ussing-chamber-TEER.title: Ussing chamber & transepithelial electrical resistance (TEER)
methods/Ussing-chamber-TEER.body_md:
# Ussing chamber & transepithelial electrical resistance (TEER)

Ex vivo electrophysiological measurement of mucosal barrier integrity. Freshly excised intestinal tissue (or a polarised cell-monolayer) is mounted between two half-chambers; resistance to applied current measures predominantly tight-junction-restricted ion flow. Lower TEER ⇒ leakier barrier.

## Why pair it with FITC-dextran

- **Mechanism vs phenotype**: FD-4 reports the in vivo whole-animal flux; TEER reports the underlying mucosal conductance on the explant. They usually agree on direction; when they disagree, the in vivo flux is also influenced by gastric emptying, motility, and luminal volume — TEER isolates the epithelial component.
- **Region resolution**: TEER lets you compare ileum vs proximal colon vs distal colon on the same animal; FD-4 is whole-gut.

## Canonical parameters

| Parameter | Typical |
|---|---|
| Tissue | freshly stripped mouse ileum or proximal colon, 0.1 cm² aperture |
| Buffer | Krebs-Ringer, oxygenated, 37 °C |
| Endpoint | baseline TEER (Ω·cm²) and probe flux (FD-4, mannitol) over 2 h |

methods/Western-blot-tight-junction.title: Western blot & immunofluorescence of tight-junction proteins
methods/Western-blot-tight-junction.body_md:
# Western blot & immunofluorescence of tight-junction proteins

Protein-level quantification of the canonical tight-junction (TJ) cassette: **ZO-1** (zonula occludens-1, scaffolding), **occludin**, **claudin-1**, **claudin-3**, **claudin-4** (sealing), and **claudin-2** (pore-forming, *up*-regulated in leaky barriers). The mechanistic substrate behind any FD-4 movement.

## Why we need it

The hypothesis posits a mechanism (LGG up-regulates ZO-1 / occludin / claudin-1/3/4 and down-regulates claudin-2). FD-4 is the phenotype; this assay is the molecular evidence that the phenotype is barrier-mediated and not, say, motility-mediated.

Two read-outs:
- **Western blot** of mucosal scrapes: bulk total protein.
- **Immunofluorescence** of frozen sections: spatial localisation. Crucial because **total ZO-1** can be unchanged while **membrane-localised ZO-1** drops by 50 % — IF catches that, WB doesn't.

## Common artefacts

- **Antibody clone effects**: claudin antibodies are notoriously clone-dependent. Always run a positive-control tissue alongside.
- **Total vs membrane pools**: bulk WB conflates the two. Pair WB with IF on adjacent sections.
- **β-actin or GAPDH normalisation**: housekeeping genes can shift in colitis. Use total-protein stain (Ponceau or stain-free) when possible.

See [[../failure-modes/ZO-1-occludin-detection-artifacts]] for the full failure-mode entry.

methods/alcohol-induced-leaky-gut-model.title: Alcohol / ethanol-induced gut barrier disruption
methods/alcohol-induced-leaky-gut-model.body_md:
# Alcohol / ethanol-induced gut barrier disruption

Acute or chronic ethanol exposure used to model alcoholic liver disease and the gut hyperpermeability that precedes it. Two dominant variants in the corpus:

- **Lieber-DeCarli liquid diet** — chronic; ethanol replaces a fraction of dietary calories.
- **NIAAA chronic-plus-binge** (Bertola, Wang, Gao) — 10 d Lieber-DeCarli followed by a single oral binge; the canonical mouse model for alcoholic liver injury, with reproducible gut barrier loss at 9 h post-binge.

## Relevance to this hypothesis

Like DSS, this is a **challenge model**, not the unchallenged-baseline design our hypothesis targets. Multiple LGG papers in our corpus (Salemi 2023, Mishra 2023, Yang 2025, Pan 2024, Zhang 2026) report that LGG (live or supernatant or EPS) **mitigates ethanol-induced FD-4 elevation and tight-junction disassembly**. None demonstrates a comparable effect in unstressed mice. See [[../failure-modes/challenge-model-vs-baseline-mismatch]].

## Canonical parameters

- **Lieber-DeCarli**: 4–6 weeks, 4 % v/v ethanol in liquid diet, pair-fed isocaloric controls.
- **NIAAA**: 10 d 5 % ethanol in liquid diet → single 5 g/kg oral gavage on day 11; sacrifice 9 h later.
- **Acute binge alone**: 6 g/kg ethanol p.o.; barrier loss measurable at 4 h.

methods/bacterial-CFU-enumeration.title: Bacterial CFU enumeration
methods/bacterial-CFU-enumeration.body_md:
# Bacterial CFU enumeration

Viable-cell counting by serial dilution and plating on selective agar. The **dosing-QC backbone** of any probiotic study — without it, you do not actually know what dose the animals received.

## Two non-negotiable measurements

1. **Dose-day CFU** — plate the gavage suspension at the moment of dosing. Anything below 80 % of the design dose (1×10⁹ CFU/mouse) means the day is uninterpretable.
2. **Engraftment CFU** — plate fresh faeces from a subset of LGG mice and a subset of controls weekly. Confirms LGG colonisation in the treatment arm and rules out cross-contamination of controls (pairs with [[16S-rRNA-microbiome-sequencing]]).

## LGG-specific protocol

- **Selective medium**: MRS agar at pH 5.5 with 0.05 % L-cysteine (suppresses common contaminants while supporting Lactobacillus); incubate **anaerobically at 37 °C for 48 h**.
- **Differentiation from endogenous Lactobacillus**: MRS alone won't tell LGG from native *L. murinus* or *L. johnsonii*. Confirm strain identity by colony PCR (LGG-specific primers, e.g. *welE*) on a sample of colonies, or by 16S sequencing.

methods/ex-vivo-intestinal-permeability-.title: Ex vivo intestinal permeability assay (X-IPA / Ussing-style)
methods/ex-vivo-intestinal-permeability-.body_md:
# Ex vivo intestinal permeability assay (X-IPA / Ussing-style)

Fresh mucosal explant assays in which a defined area of intestine is mounted between donor (luminal) and acceptor (serosal) chambers, FD-4 is added apically, and acceptor fluorescence is read across hours. Aldars-García et al. 2023 (in our protocols corpus) standardise this as "X-IPA".

## Why we'd add it

X-IPA dissects what FD-4 in vivo cannot:

- **Region resolution**: per-segment ileum vs proximal vs distal colon on the same animal.
- **Dynamics**: real-time accumulation curves rather than a single 4-h plasma timepoint.
- **Mechanism overlay**: the same mounted tissue can be probed pharmacologically (e.g. claudin-2 inhibitors) to test specific pathways.

## When to skip it

For a binary "does LGG lower in vivo permeability by 30 %?" answer, X-IPA is overkill. It belongs in a **mechanism follow-up** experiment after the in vivo readout is positive.

## Sources mentioning this

- [[../sources/2023-aldarsgarca-ex-vivo-intestinal-permeability-assay|2023 · Aldars-García — Ex vivo intestinal permeability assay (X-IPA) for tracking barrier function dynamics]]
- [[../sources/2022-llerena-testing-epithelial-permeability-fetal-tissue|2022 · Llerena — Testing Epithelial Permeability in Fetal Tissue-derived Enteroids]]
- [[../sources/2021-boerner-functional-assessment-intestinal-permeability-neutrophil|2021 · Boerner — Functional Assessment of Intestinal Permeability and Neutrophil Transepithelial Migration in Mice]]

methods/high-fat-diet-model.title: High-fat diet (HFD) gut-barrier model
methods/high-fat-diet-model.body_md:
# High-fat diet (HFD) gut-barrier model

Mice fed 45–60 % kcal-from-fat purified diets for 8–16 weeks develop diet-induced obesity, metabolic endotoxaemia, and a measurable, slow-onset increase in intestinal permeability. The canonical pairing is **Research Diets D12492 (60 % kcal fat)** vs the matched D12450J low-fat control.

## Relevance to this hypothesis

A second challenge model (after DSS and alcohol) in which LGG has been shown to attenuate barrier dysfunction (Arellano-García 2023/2025, Pan 2024, Wang 2025 MASLD/MASH). Its kinetics are slow (weeks) and the effect size is subtler than DSS — closer to the magnitude one would expect under a 30 % reduction threshold in unstressed mice.

If Pass 3 chooses a challenge variant, **HFD is the closest in physiology to a "naturally permeability-shifted" baseline** — the hyperpermeability is mild, chronic, microbiome-driven, and clinically translatable, unlike the brutal acute injury of DSS.

## Canonical parameters

| Parameter | Typical |
|---|---|
| Diet | Research Diets D12492 (60 % kcal fat) or D12451 (45 %) |
| Duration | 8–16 weeks |
| Endpoint | body weight, glucose tolerance, plasma LBP / LPS, FD-4, hepatic triglyceride |

methods/oral-gavage-mouse.title: Oral gavage in mice
methods/oral-gavage-mouse.body_md:
# Oral gavage in mice

Direct intragastric delivery of a defined volume / dose using a curved, ball-tipped feeding needle. The standard route for both the **probiotic intervention** (LGG suspension) and the **assay reagent** (FITC-dextran) in this hypothesis.

## Why we use it (vs drinking-water dosing)

- **Dose certainty**: drinking-water dosing depends on water intake — variable across mice and across cages, and probiotic viability decays over hours in water bottles at cage temperature.
- **Synchrony**: every animal is dosed within minutes of every other, which matters for time-point readouts (FD-4 plasma at 4 h post-gavage).
- **Single-strain**: avoids cross-contamination of control cages via shared water lines.

## Canonical parameters (mouse)

| Parameter | Typical | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Needle | 22G, 1.5", curved, stainless ball-tip (e.g. Kent Scientific FNS-22-1.5) | size up to 18G for chronic studies |
| Volume | 100–200 µL (≤ 10 mL/kg per IACUC norms) | larger volumes risk reflux & aspiration |
| Restraint | gentle scruff, head-tilt, immobile tail | training is rate-limiting; first 2 weeks of any LGG study should be under a senior tech |
| Frequency | once daily, same hour | LGG colonisation is transient; daily dosing required to maintain CFU |

## LGG-specific considerations

- **Prepare the dose on the day**, not the day before. Frozen LGG glycerol stock → MRS broth → log-phase growth → wash in PBS → resuspend at 5×10⁹ CFU/mL → 200 µL = 1×10⁹ CFU/mouse.
- **Confirm CFU on the dose-day plate**: serial-dilute the dosing suspension and plate on MRS agar at the time of gavage. Without this, viability loss between thaw and dosing is invisible. See [[../failure-modes/LGG-viability-loss-during-gavage-prep]].
- **Cold chain**: keep the gavage syringe-pool on ice; warm individual aliquots only at the moment of dosing. Room-temperature LGG suspensions lose ~0.5–1 log of viability per hour.

## FD-4 gavage specifics

- Fast 6 h prior; water ad lib. See [[../failure-modes/incomplete-fasting-before-gavage]].
- Wrap dosing tubes in foil to prevent FITC photobleaching. See [[../failure-modes/FITC-dextran-photobleaching]].

organisms/C57BL-6.title: Mus musculus, C57BL/6 inbred strain

characteristics: inbred mouse strain, the most-used genetic background in immunology and gut-microbiome research
C57BL/6J carries a 5-exon Nnt deletion (mitochondrial NADP(H) regeneration) absent in C57BL/6N — small but reproducible metabolic differences
both substrains develop measurable DSS, alcohol, and HFD-induced gut barrier loss; baseline FD-4 is low and tightly distributed
microbiome composition shifts measurably between vendors (JAX, Taconic, Charles River, Envigo) — same genotype, different commensals

organisms/C57BL-6.body_md:
# Mus musculus, C57BL/6 inbred strain

The hypothesis pre-registers **C57BL/6J** specifically (JAX 000664) — not the generic "C57BL/6" — for two reasons:

1. The **C57BL/6J vs C57BL/6N substrain confounder** is real: the *Nnt* deletion in 6J alters mitochondrial NADP(H) cycling and downstream redox tone; documented baseline microbiome and metabolic differences from 6N. Mixing substrains across arms (or vs published literature using the other) silently invalidates comparisons. See [[../failure-modes/C57BL-6J-vs-N-substrain-confounder]].
2. The **vendor-of-origin microbiome baseline** matters: JAX, Taconic, and Charles River mice arrive with measurably different commensal communities. Two LGG studies on "C57BL/6 mice" from different vendors are not the same experiment. See [[../failure-modes/vendor-of-origin-microbiome-baseline]].

## Operational notes (this hypothesis)

- **Source**: Jackson 000664 (C57BL/6J), single shipment, single age cohort.
- **Sex / age**: both sexes; 6–10 weeks at study start.
- **Acclimation**: ≥ 14 d in the local vivarium before any intervention — enough for the microbiome to re-equilibrate to local feed / water / housing.
- **Cohousing**: LGG and control arms in **separate cages** with sentinel monitoring; ideally separate ventilated racks. Coprophagy across cages collapses the LGG-vs-control contrast — see [[../failure-modes/coprophagy-microbiome-equilibration]].

organisms/Caco-2.title: Caco-2 (human colorectal carcinoma → intestinal-epithelial-cell model)

characteristics: spontaneously polarises into a tight, brush-border-bearing monolayer over ~21 d on Transwell inserts
the standard in vitro intestinal-epithelial-cell model for TEER and permeability

organisms/Caco-2.body_md:
# Caco-2 (human colorectal carcinoma → intestinal-epithelial-cell model)

Caco-2 monolayers on Transwell inserts are the in vitro workhorse for tight-junction biology — TEER, FD-4 flux, claudin localisation under cytokine challenge. Several mechanism-side papers in our corpus (Yan/Polk follow-ups, Domínguez-Díaz 2023, Hu 2024) use Caco-2 as the in vitro complement to in vivo work.

## What Caco-2 won't tell you

- Real microbiota interactions (no ecology, no commensal context).
- Mucus layer (Caco-2 lacks goblet-cell phenotype unless co-cultured with HT29-MTX).
- Strain colonisation kinetics in a complex community.

For this hypothesis (in vivo FD-4 readout) Caco-2 work is supportive mechanism evidence only.

organisms/lactobacillus-rhamnosus-GG.title: *Lacticaseibacillus rhamnosus* GG (LGG / ATCC 53103 / DSM 33156)

characteristics: human-origin (isolated from a healthy adult faecal sample by Goldin & Gorbach, 1985)
taxonomy reassigned to Lacticaseibacillus rhamnosus in 2020 — most recent papers use the new name; older work uses Lactobacillus
facultatively heterofermentative; grows on MRS at 37 °C; microaerotolerant
characteristic SpaCBA pili (msa locus) mediate mucus and intestinal-cell adhesion
secretes p40 (Msp1) and p75 (Msp2) soluble proteins implicated in barrier protection

organisms/lactobacillus-rhamnosus-GG.body_md:
# *Lacticaseibacillus rhamnosus* GG (LGG / ATCC 53103 / DSM 33156)

The most thoroughly studied probiotic *Lactobacillus*, isolated by Sherwood Gorbach and Barry Goldin in 1985 from a healthy adult human stool sample. Reclassified in 2020 from *Lactobacillus rhamnosus* GG to *Lacticaseibacillus rhamnosus* GG; both names appear interchangeably across our corpus.

## Why this strain (vs other Lactobacilli)

- **Mechanistic depth**: the p40 / EGFR / PI3K-Akt axis (Yan/Polk lineage) is the most thoroughly characterised single-strain barrier mechanism in the probiotic literature.
- **Adhesion**: the **SpaCBA pili** explain why LGG transiently colonises the human / mouse gut better than most lab Lactobacilli — relevant to whether 4 weeks of daily dosing achieves a colonised steady-state vs cleared-by-day-3 transit.
- **Commercial validation**: human-grade preparations (Culturelle) have decades of safety data (with rare exceptions — see [[../failure-modes/probiotic-bacteremia-sepsis]]).
- **Commons reuse**: LGG appears in many GROUNDWORK hypotheses (gut-brain axis, NEC, colitis, allergic disease) — this organism page is a strong promotion candidate for `commons/organisms/`.

## Sourcing for this hypothesis

- **ATCC 53103** is the reference. Order a sealed master vial; expand once into a working glycerol stock; freeze in single-use cryoaliquots at −80 °C. **Do not** propagate from culture-to-culture beyond 5 passages between cryostock and dosing, to avoid drift. See [[../failure-modes/LGG-strain-drift-passage]].
- **DSM 33156** at DSMZ is equivalent.
- **Commercial probiotic capsules** (e.g. Culturelle) are NOT acceptable as study material — undefined dose, unknown carrier load, batch-to-batch variability.

## Dose for this hypothesis

- **1 × 10⁹ CFU / mouse / day** (200 µL of a 5 × 10⁹ CFU/mL suspension in PBS) by oral gavage for 28 d.
- Confirm CFU on the dose-day plate; see [[../methods/bacterial-CFU-enumeration]].

## Heat-killed / postbiotic LGG

A consistent secondary finding across our corpus is that **heat-killed LGG retains substantial barrier-protective activity** (Le Noci 2022, Arellano-García 2023/2025, Oglio 2025/2026, Domínguez-Díaz 2023). Mechanism: p40, EPS, and EVs are all heat-stable. Pass 3 should consider a heat-killed-LGG arm as the cleanest test of viability dependence.

## Cross-strain context

Other *L. rhamnosus* strains (BL23, HN001, GR-1) appear in the corpus — see [[lactobacillus-rhamnosus-other-strains]]. Some core mechanisms (p40/p75 in *L. casei* BL23, Bäuerl 2010) are conserved; specific findings should NOT be assumed to transfer from other strains to LGG without explicit testing.

organisms/lactobacillus-rhamnosus-other-.body_md:
# *Lactobacillus rhamnosus* — non-GG strains

Other *L. rhamnosus* strains appear scattered through our corpus in two contexts:

1. **As comparators** to LGG within the same study (less common in our papers).
2. **As substitutes** in studies that simply say "Lactobacillus rhamnosus" without strain identification — these should be treated as **weak evidence for LGG specifically**.

## Strain-conservation evidence

- Bäuerl 2010 showed orthologous p40/p75 from *L. casei* BL23 (sister species) have similar barrier-protective activity — suggesting the soluble-protein mechanism is at least genus-conserved.
- The 2014 Singh et al. retraction (LGG fermented milk in aging mice) used "Lactobacillus rhamnosus" without strain ID — part of why it was retracted.

## What to do for this hypothesis

The hypothesis pre-registers **LGG / ATCC 53103 specifically**. Cross-strain papers in this wiki are useful background but **must not be cited as direct evidence for the hypothesis**.
